food_subgroup: Mashed leafy greens with lentils and tempering

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Keerai Masiyal. Nearest parent is a cooked leafy vegetable with legume dish. A new concept for South Indian mashed spinach preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked spinach and lentil mash, South Indian style (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side serving of keerai masiyal is approximately 150g — a dark-green, smooth, thick mash of cooked spinach combined with soft toor or moong dal, finished with a ghee-garlic tempering.

nutrient_source.title: Keerai Masiyal — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from cooked spinach and toor/moong dal component data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone keerai masiyal entry. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked spinach and toor/moong dal composition data per standard recipe proportions (spinach ~55%, dal ~20%, oil/ghee ~6%, water/spices balance).

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from cooked spinach and lentil mixture; very high fibre and protein content gives very low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 278mg per serving (150g) — moderate; the very high potassium (~480mg per serving) from spinach and dal provides excellent blood pressure benefit.
hypertension.guidance: Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — the potassium-sodium ratio is highly favourable. Spinach is one of the best dietary sources of potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~32); glycaemic load per 150g serving is approximately 5 — very low. High fibre, protein, and magnesium from spinach and dal are beneficial for insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Highly recommended for diabetic patients — very low GI and GL, high in fibre and magnesium which improves insulin sensitivity. Regular consumption of leafy greens is strongly associated with reduced diabetes risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Spinach is one of the richest sources of folate (~88mcg DFE per 100g) and vitamin A in South Indian cuisine; turmeric provides curcumin; iron is a concern for patients with active inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the folate content supports cell repair, vitamin A supports immune function, and turmeric provides anti-inflammatory benefits. The ghee tempering provides fat-soluble vitamin absorption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keerai Masiyal is a traditional South Indian Tamil dish of cooked, mashed spinach (keerai) combined with soft toor dal or moong dal into a thick, smooth, dark-green mash. The dish is finished with a hot ghee tempering of mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and garlic, which adds fragrance and depth. It is one of the most iron- and folate-dense side dishes in South Indian cuisine. Eaten as a side with plain rice and rasam as part of a Tamil home meal. High in calcium, iron, vitamin A, and folate — nutritionally exceptional despite its simple preparation.
common_user_queries: South Indian spinach curry iron content

confidence_reason: No standalone keerai masiyal entry in ICMR-NIN. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked spinach and dal component data. Spinach nutrition values are well-established; estimates are reliable.
notes: Contains ghee (dairy) — not vegan or dairy-free when made with ghee; vegan when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 7.5mg per serving (150g) when made with ghee. Oxalate content of spinach may reduce calcium bioavailability — calcium from other foods in the meal is better absorbed. Iron is non-heme iron (plant source) — pair with vitamin C-rich foods to enhance absorption.
